coredata.dc:title: An “on-off-on” fluorescent system based on the microwave-assisted preparation of copper-functionalized carbon quantum dots for sensitive detection of ascorbic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yang
Chen, Yu
Mo, Wanqi
Li, Xiaobai
Ma, Hongwei
Zhao, Xiaohong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are a unique class of fascinating carbon-based fluorescent sensors with strong photoluminescence, chemical stability and good biocompatibility. In this work, new copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) were successfully synthesized from citric acid, ammonium tartrate and CuCl2 via a household microwave. The Cu-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emissions, high fluorescence quantum yield (~34.74%) and excellent fluorescence stability. The fluorescence of Cu-CDs can be directly quenched by ascorbic acid (AA) linearly in the range of 0.1–3.0 μM based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The limit of detection (LOD) can be determined to 27 nM. Meanwhile, an “on-off-on” sensing system based on the complex of Cu-CDs and Fe3+ is designed and served as highly specific detection probes for AA through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with a lower LOD of 6 nM, which is one of the best reports. The proposed methods provide obvious advantages of convenience, simplicity, rapid response, high selectivity and sensitivity. Besides, good results also have been acquired for AA detection in real medicine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
Fluorescence “on-off-on” system
Carbon dots
Optical sensing
Sustainable sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for the financial support by the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project for Heilongjiang Provincial College Students ( 41111217 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51903031 ), Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST ( 2019QNRC001 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project ( 2019T120249 , 2018M630331 ), Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Fund ( LBH-Z18010 , LBH-TZ1001 ), Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province ( YQ2019E003 ), the 111 Project ( B20088 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of the Cu-CDs; (b) High-resolution TEM images of the Cu-CDs; (c) Size distribution of the Cu-CDs. Insert: Freeze-dried Cu-CDs powder after purification; (d) FT-IR spectrum of the Cu-CDs.
(a) XPS spectrum of Cu-CDs; (b–d) High-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s, N1s and Cu2p for the Cu-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation (λ em = 440 nm) and emission (λ ex = 350 nm) spectra of the Cu-CDs. The insert displays the photograph of the as-prepared Cu-CDs aqueous solution under 365 nm UV illumination; (b) In the UV–vis spectrum, the linear relationship between the Cu-CDs solution and AA (0–60 μM); (c) Fluorescence spectra of the Cu-CDs at different λ ex from 300 to 400 nm; (d) Fluorescence spectra of CDs undoped Cu2+ at different λ ex from 300 to 400 nm; (e) The fluorescence emission spectra of the Cu-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 30.8 μM. Inset: the relationship between the 1-F/F0 value and the concentration of Fe3+ (F and F0 represent the PL intensities of the Cu-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+); (f) PL decay spectra of the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ system.

(a–b) Normalized intensity of the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ dispersed in different pH solutions. The fluorescence properties of the Cu-CDs were investigated by continuous ultraviolet irradiation (c), temperature (d), NaCl concentrations (e) and the Cu-CDs concentration (5–50 mg/mL) (f).

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of the Cu-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of AA ranging from 0 to 90.8 μM. Insert: The linear relationship graph between the F/F0 value and the concentrations of AA; (b) PL decay spectra of the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-AA; (c) After adding 20 μM Fe3+ to the Cu-CDs solution, the fluorescent intensity was enhanced as different concentrations of AA increase. Inset: The linear relationship between the F/F0 value and the concentration of AA; (d) PL decay spectra of the Cu-CDs-Fe3+ and Cu-CDs-Fe3+-AA.
PL intensities of the Cu-CDs probe (a) and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ system (b) containing different kinds of other physiological molecules (the concentration of physiological molecules and amino acids is 3.0 μM).
The schematic illustration of the preparation of the Cu-CDs and the systems of direct detection and “on-off-on” detection of AA.
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ detection.
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for AA detection.

Multi-exponential decay curves of Cu-CDs, Cu-CDs-Fe3+, Cu-CDs-AA and Cu-CDs-Fe3+-AA solutions, respectively. τi: the corresponding lifetime of different components, B: the corresponding pre-exponential factor, τav: the average lifetime.
Determination of AA in different water samples by using Cu-CDs-Fe3+-AA and Cu-CDs-AA fluorescence sensing system.

head.ce:title: An “on-off-on” fluorescent system based on the microwave-assisted preparation of copper-functionalized carbon quantum dots for sensitive detection of ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Ministry of Education, Engineering Research Center of Forest Bio-Preparation, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, China

Engineering Research Center of Forest Bio-Preparation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Engineering Laboratory of Bio-Resource Eco-Utilization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a unique class of fascinating carbon-based fluorescent sensors with strong photoluminescence, chemical stability and good biocompatibility. In this work, new copper-doped carbon dots (Cu-CDs) were successfully synthesized from citric acid, ammonium tartrate and CuCl2 via a household microwave. The Cu-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emissions, high fluorescence quantum yield (~34.74%) and excellent fluorescence stability. The fluorescence of Cu-CDs can be directly quenched by ascorbic acid (AA) linearly in the range of 0.1–3.0 μM based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The limit of detection (LOD) can be determined to 27 nM. Meanwhile, an “on-off-on” sensing system based on the complex of Cu-CDs and Fe3+ is designed and served as highly specific detection probes for AA through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with a lower LOD of 6 nM, which is one of the best reports. The proposed methods provide obvious advantages of convenience, simplicity, rapid response, high selectivity and sensitivity. Besides, good results also have been acquired for AA detection in real medicine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA, also known as vitamin C) is a key trace organic substance that plays a very important role in many biochemical processes of humans [1]. AA not only participates in the formation of intercellular substances, but also maintains the normal functions of teeth, bones, blood vessels and muscles. Besides, AA is directly involved in the synthesis of many small biomolecules in the human body, such as adenosine triphosphate (ATP), peptide hormones and dopamine. More importantly, the content of AA in the body directly affects human health. The shortage of AA in the body will cause severe scurvy, while the excessive intake of AA will cause stones, anemia and gout [2]. The Food and Nutrition Committee of the National Institute of Medicine recommended that the average daily AA intake for male and female adults is 90 mg and 70 mg, respectively. In addition, the tolerable limit of daily intake for men and women is estimated to be 2000 mg per day. Therefore, high-sensitivity and precise detection of AA is very significant in the pharmaceutical, clinical and food industries.

Traditional detection methods for AA including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], titration analysis [4], spectrophotometry [5], electrochemistry [6] and chromatography [7]. Although these methods have made some progress in sensitivity and selectivity to AA, the inherent shortcomings of expensive instrumentation, complex sample preparation procedures, long-time consumption and low throughput immensely limit their further applications in real-time fields. Compared to other technologies, the fluorescence detection method has attracted widespread attention because of its low cost, high sensitivity and suitability for on-site detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new fluorescent carbon-based nano-material, that are supposed to be rival alternatives to traditional fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots due to their unique properties including good hydrophilicity, easy functionalization, excellent chemical stability, admirable biocompatibility and easy preparation [8]. CDs have been widely applied in many fields, such as bioimaging [9], photoelectric [10], photocatalysis [11], drug delivery [12], biomolecule detection [13] and fluorescence sensing [14]. Although promising, the disadvantages of the multi-step chemical modification, expensive raw materials, time-consuming and complicated instruments have become the bottleneck for CDs applications, the development of an efficient and low-cost method for the preparation of CDs has aroused research interests. Further, fluorescence quenching as the sensing signal has been widely applied in the fields of CDs-based fluorescence sensing. However, unpredictable interference in complex environments can lead to higher false alarm rates.

In this work, Cu2+ functionalized carbon dots (Cu-CDs) were synthesized through a simple and efficient microwave-assisted method (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence intensity of the Cu-CDs can be directly quenched by AA linearly with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 27 nM. The quenching mechanism can be attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). Meanwhile, the Cu-CDs-Fe3+ complex as an “on-off-on” specific detection probe is developed for AA detection with a lower LOD of 6 nM, which is one of the best reports. Therefore, the two systems of direct detection and “on-off-on” detection to identify AA can effectively achieve selective detection of AA. This strategy provides a new idea for the future development of AA sensors.

All chemicals and solvents used are of analytical grade and do not need any further purification. CuCl2·2H2O and other soluble metal ion salts were purchased from the Tianjin Jinko Institute of Refined Chemical Engineering. Citric acid and ammonium tartrate were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corp (Beijing, China). Urea and various amino acids were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. High-purity water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was obtained from a MilliQ water purification system (Millipore, MA, USA). The vitamin C pills to be tested were purchased from Jilin City Luwang Pharmaceutical Co, Ltd. The microwave oven used in the experiment was purchased from Galanz.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum was recorded on a Shimadzu XRD-6100 spectrometer (Kyoto, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) with a step distance of 0.04° and a range of 5°–60°. The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was performed on the Thermo Fisher K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The morphology of Cu-CDs was observed on a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, JEM-2100) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Before the TEM analysis, the sample was dispersed in high-purity water and then dropped onto a 300-mesh copper grid coated with a thin carbon film. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained by the KBr particle method on a Nicolet FT-IR 360 spectrometer with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence measurement was performed on a Shimadzu RF-6000 PC fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a xenon lamp at room temperature. Throughout the experiment, a quartz cuvette with an optical path of 1 cm was used, and the slit width of the excitation and emission spectra was set to 5 nm.

CuCl2·2H2O (0.5 g), citric acid (0.5 g) and ammonium nitrate (1.0 g) were dissolved in 35 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 10 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a domestic microwave oven kept at 750 W for 15 min. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the final products were collected by centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis for 2 days (cutoff Mn: 500 Da). The purified Cu-CDs solution was freeze-dried into powder (Fig. 1 c insert) and 50.0 mg/mL of the Cu-CDs were prepared for stock solution.

The typical procedure for AA and Fe3+ detection was performed in deionized water (pH 7.0) at room temperature as follows. 3.0 μL AA solutions were added into 3.0 mL Cu-CDs blank solution and then the mixture was thoroughly mixed for 3 min, the fluorescence spectrum was measured at the excited wavelength of 350 nm. Both the slit widths of excitation and emission were 5 nm.

To avoid interference from other possible cations, the 13 types of cations (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe3+,
NH 4 +
, Al3+) and small bio-molecules (including urea and other amino acids) were also investigated. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded before and after adding any cations into the Cu-CDs solution.

For direct AA detection, 3 μL different amounts of AA were added into the 3 mL Cu-CDs solution and the final concentrations of AA could range from 0 to 90.8 μM. For the “on-off-on” fluorescence probes, Fe3+ was added to the Cu-CDs sensing system and labeled as a Cu-CDs-Fe3+ sensing detector. Different amounts of AA were added into the Cu-CDs-Fe3+ system and the final concentrations of AA could range from 0 to 4.2 μM. These mixtures were allowed to stand for 5 min and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded. Then the enhanced efficiency of fluorescence was calculated.

The selectivity test of the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ sensing systems to AA was carried out. First, the optimized sensing system of 3 mL of the 50 mg/mL Cu-CDs was quenched by 20 μL FeCl3 (0.1 M). Then, the fluorescence intensity of the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ was recorded before and after the addition of 200 μL interfering substances, including some cations, amino acids and possible interfering small molecules.

To evaluate the feasibility of the detection for AA in actual tablet samples using the new Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ sensing systems, standard addition methods were carried out to directly analyze the AA in tablets. The proportion of the pill was about 50 mg/0.2 g tablet. One pill was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and diluted 100 times for use. Typically, 0.5 μL of the sample solution was added to the Cu-CDs solution and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ sensing systems, respectively. The resulting solution was mixed well and kept at room temperature for 5 min. Then the fluorescence spectrum was recorded under 350 nm excitation. The content of AA in the actual sample was calculated according to the linear equation obtained from the AA standard solution.

The morphology and size distribution of the Cu-CDs were characterized by TEM. The Cu-CDs particles are nearly spherical in shape (Fig. 1a), and the lattice spacing is 0.209 nm which is close to the (100) crystal facet of graphite carbon (Fig. 1b). The narrow sizes of the Cu-CDs are distributed ranging from 0.5 to 3.5 nm at an average size of 2.345 nm (Fig. 1c). The FT-IR spectroscopy is conducted to analyze the surface functional groups of the Cu-CDs in Fig. 1d. The wide absorption peaks at 3173 cm−1 and 3052 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of the O–H and N–H groups. The strong absorption peaks of 1957 cm−1 and 1408 cm−1 provide the evidence of the CO and CC groups. The mixed peaks at 1250 - 1400 cm−1 correspond to the aromatic nitrogen heterocycles and stretching vibrations of C–O groups. The characteristic peaks around 800 - 1000 cm−1 represent the presence of Cu–COOH and Cu–NH2 groups [15,16]. These results indicate that the copper-doped CDs were successfully prepared. In addition, the functional groups confirmed on the surface can improve the hydrophilicity of the Cu-CDs, which is conducive to the detection of aqueous samples.

XPS analysis was carried out to investigate the composition and surface state of the Cu-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, their XPS survey spectrum shows four main features of C1s, O1s, N1s and Cu2p, and the contents of C, O, N and Cu are determined to be 44.64%, 40.33%, 12.01% and 3.01%, respectively. To be more specific, the typical C1s spectrum shows the presence of C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, CN and CO groups, which are located at 284.0, 284.7, 285.9 and 287.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b). The typical N1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks at 400.9, 400.0 and 399.2 eV, corresponding to the CN–C, C–N and N–H groups, respectively (Fig. 2c). Again, the partial XPS spectrum of Cu2p (Fig. 2d) exhibits two peaks at 934.1 and 954.1 eV, which are assigned to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Cu [17,18]. Thus, the existence of the Cu element reveals that Cu has been well functionalized on the surface of the Cu-CDs. These findings suggested that the results of XPS are consistent with that of the FT-IR, which are further confirmed that these functional groups are present on the surface of the Cu-CDs.

The optical properties of the Cu-CDs were investigated and shown in Fig. 3 a. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the absorption center at 350 nm is mainly attributed to the n-π* energy transitions of the Cu-CDs, indicating the presence of the energy trap stemmed from surface carboxylic groups (C–O or CO) [19]. Maximum excitation and emission are recorded at around 350 nm and 440 nm, respectively, with a Stokes shift of 90 nm. As shown in Fig. 3a insert, the Cu-CDs solution emits bright blue fluorescent under UV illumination (365 nm). When different concentrations (0–80 μM) of Fe3+ are added to the Cu-CDs solution, Fe3+ can coordinate with the hydroxyl groups of the Cu-CDs and form a complex, resulting in significant decrease of absorbance at around 350 nm with a good linear relationship (Fig. 3b). Further, it can be seen from Fig. 3c that the Cu-CDs display obvious excitation-dependent emissions. The emission spectra of the as-synthesized Cu-CDs shift from 430 nm to 460 nm in the PL intensity as the excitation wavelength varied from 300 nm to 400 nm. This excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence is due to the surface state affecting the bandgap of the Cu-CDs [20]. For comparison, the CDs synthesized by citric acid and ammonium nitrate undoped CuCl2 were prepared under the same experimental conditions. The CDs undoped CuCl2 also show good optical properties and the emission maximum shifted to a higher wavelength as the excitation wavelength increased (Fig. 3d). It is generally agreed that the excitation-dependent emissions may result from the appearance of various sizes and multiple surface trap status of the CDs. The Cu-CDs show better fluorescence properties than the CDs undoped CuCl2 and the quantum yield (QY) of the Cu-CDs is 34.74%, which is higher than the CDs undoped CuCl2. Thus, it is hypothesized that there are non-radiative transitions between the excited electrons due to the introduced Cu2+ modification of the surface states [15]. In order to obtain the “on-off-on” sensing system, the fluorescence emission spectra of the Cu-CDs for quantitative detection of Fe3+ in ultrapure water (pH 7.0) were carried out (Fig. 3e). An obvious decrease in fluorescence intensity of the Cu-CDs was shown as the increased concentration of Fe3+ and a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.993) between the fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration was observed from 0.1 to 1.0 μM (Fig. 3e insert). Based on Stern-Volmer plot analysis, the linear equation can be fitted as:
1 − F /
F 0
=
0.01
+
0.11
c
F
e
3 +
( μ M )

Where F and F0 are the PL intensities of the Cu-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ separately. The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ is calculated to be 12 nM. As the absorption spectrum of the Cu-CDs-Fe3+ system overlaps with the emission spectrum of the Cu-CDs (Fig. 3a), and the average fluorescence lifetime of the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ were measured to be 5.46 ns and 3.86 ns, respectively (Fig. 3f). Therefore, the mechanism of the observed fluorescence quenching performance should be regulated to the FRET. Compared with other methods, our established method provides comparable and higher linearity ranges and detection limits (Table 1 ). The fluorescence intensity of the Cu-CDs is gradually decreased with the addition of Fe3+ and reaches a plateau at about 30 μM Fe3+. Excess Fe3+ in water solution will pollute the environment, and the quenching ability of the Cu-CDs is similar between 30 μM and 20 μM. Therefore, 20 μM Fe3+ is chosen for AA detection that Cu-CDs fluorescence is completely turned off while not in excess.

Then, the effects of pH, continuous UV exposure, concentrations of NaCl, temperature and concentrations of the Cu-CDs on the fluorescence properties of the Cu-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the pH dependence of the fluorescence intensity was studied in the pH range of 1–14. For the Cu-CDs, as varying pH from 1 to 5, the fluorescent intensity displays an obvious increase and is almost stable in the range of 5–12. Thereafter, the intensity decreases gradually (Fig. 4a). For the Cu-CDs-Fe3+ sensing system, as varying pH from 1 to 7, the fluorescent intensity displays an obvious increase and the intensity decreases gradually (Fig. 4b). The pH-sensitive feature is attributed to the reversible protonation of surface-functionalized groups in acid and alkaline medium, which is relevant to the fluorescence emission of the Cu-CDs [15]. More importantly, there is no obvious change in both fluorescence peak positions and intensities after continuous UV illumination for 80 min (Fig. 4c), which indicates that both the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ have high photostability. The increase of NaCl in the Cu-CDs solution contributes to no obvious change in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4d). When the temperature varies from 20 °C to 80 °C, the fluorescence intensity decreases slightly (Fig. 4e). The fluorescence intensity increases gradually with the increase of the concentration of the Cu-CDs (Fig. 4f).

The fluorescence emission spectra of the Cu-CDs for quantitative detection of AA in ultrapure water (pH 7.0) were recorded. As Fig. 5 a shown, the fluorescence intensity was quenched as the increased addition of AA and a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) between the fluorescence intensity and AA concentration is observed from 0.25 to 3.0 μM (Fig. 5a insert). Based on Stern-Volmer plot analysis, the linear equation can be fitted as:
1 − F /
F 0
=
0.0066
+
0.052
c
A A
( μ M )
.

The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 27 nM. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is mainly associated with static quenching effect (SQE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [27]. The absorption spectrum of the Cu-CDs-AA overlaps with the excitation band of Cu-CDs, and the average fluorescence lifetime of the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-AA is measured to be 5.46 ns and 5.21 ns, respectively (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the mechanism of the observed fluorescence quenching performance should be regulated to the IFE.

Under the optimized experiment conditions, the fluorescence emission spectra of the Cu-CDs-Fe3+ for quantitative detection of AA in ultrapure water (pH 7.0) are recorded. Compared with the AA directly detected by Cu-CDs, the fluorescent intensity of Cu-CDs-Fe3+ can be enhanced after the addition of the AA (Fig. 5c). The “on-off-on” detection system exhibit a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.993) between the fluorescence intensity and AA concentration from 0.1 to 0.7 μM (Fig. 5c insert) Based on Stern-Volmer plot analysis, the linear equation can be fitted as:
F /
F 0
=
0.85
+
0.21
c
A A
( μ M )
.

For an excellent sensing probe, it must be ensured that it is selective for the target analyte without other interference and possible false positives. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the selectivity of the Cu-CDs probe to other possible interfering substances (amino acids and other biomolecules) during the direct detection of AA was also studied. When the final concentration is 40 μM, most of the interfering substances tested will not cause obvious quenching or enhancement of fluorescence intensity. Therefore, as shown in Fig. 6b, the selectivity of the Cu-CDs-Fe3+ system to other possible interference substances (amino acids and other biological molecules) was also studied. At a final concentration of 20 μM, the fluorescent intensity of Cu-CDs-Fe3+ systems is higher than the initial intensity, which is the most sensitive and selective to AA. However, most of the interfering substances will not cause significant quenching or enhancement of the fluorescence intensity. Compared with other test interference species, it is found that the presence of cysteine has the greatest impact on AA detection. Despite this finding, our target real samples (such as supplement tablets) usually have little or no cysteine. Therefore, the selectivity of AA detection is still effective for medicine science applications.

In order to verify the practical applicability of direct detection and the “on-off-on” system, the detection of AA in actual drugs was performed. The selected samples are vitamin C pills purchased on the market, and the ingredients of each tablet contain 50 mg vitamin C. Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs-Fe3+ fluorescence sensor system was evaluated and verified. The results are summarized in Table 4 . These results prove that this Cu-CDs-AA and Cu-CDs-Fe3+-AA-based fluorescence “on-off-on” sensing system has the potentials to be applied for actual sample analysis. The combination of the two is expected to develop a highly specific fluorescence detector for AA detection.

In summary, simple fluorescent Cu-CDs were successfully synthesized via a high efficiency and low-cost microwave method. The obtained Cu-CDs have good crystal structures and abundant surface functional groups, which avoids tedious surface modifications in its further applications. Then, two systems of direct detection and “on-off-on” detection based on Cu-CDs were proposed to identify AA with a good linear relationship, ranging from 0.1 to 0.7 μM and 0.1–3.0 μM, respectively. Compared with previous methods, the Cu-CDs-based fluorescence sensing system has good selectivity, high sensitivity and quick response for AA detection. In addition, good results also have been acquired for AA detection in real medicine samples, which indicates that the detection systems have excellent practical applicability.

Yang Lin: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yu Chen: Validation, Writing – original draft. Wanqi Mo: Formal analysis, Visualization. Xiaobai Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Hongwei Ma: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiaohong Zhao: Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support by the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project for Heilongjiang Provincial College Students (41111217), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51903031), Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST (2019QNRC001), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (2019T120249, 2018M630331), Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Fund (LBH-Z18010, LBH-TZ1001), Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (YQ2019E003), the 111 Project (B20088).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared graphene quantum dots-based two-photon nanoprobe for direct bioimaging of endogenous ascorbic acid in living cells
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Feng L, Wu Y, Zhang D, Hu X, Zhang J, Wang P, et al. Near-Infrared Graphene Quantum Dots-Based Two-Photon Nanoprobe for Direct Bioimaging of Endogenous Ascorbic Acid in Living Cells. Anal Chem 2017; 89: 4077-4084.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Label-free simultaneous analysis of Fe(III) and ascorbic acid using fluorescence switching of ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Guo X, Yue G, Huang J, Liu C, Zeng Q, Wang L. Label-Free Simultaneous Analysis of Fe(III) and Ascorbic Acid Using Fluorescence Switching of Ultrathin Graphitic Carbon Nitride Nanosheets. ACS Appl Mater Inter 2018; 10: 26118-26172.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous derivatization and quantification of the nitric oxide metabolites nitrite and nitrate in biological fluids by gas chromatography/mass spectrometry
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tsikas D. Simultaneous derivatization and quantification of the nitric oxide metabolites nitrite and nitrate in biological fluids by gas chromatography/mass spectrometry. Anal Chem 2000; 72: 4064-4072.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sequential injection redox or acid-base titration for determination of ascorbic acid or acetic acid
Talanta
